question.english: What is it like to be a professional athlete?
question.chinese: 成為一名職業運動員是怎樣的感覺？

source_lines.text: Topic: What is it like to be a professional athlete?
主題：成為一名職業運動員是怎樣的感覺？

components.english: I think being a professional athlete is exciting and rewarding, but also incredibly demanding.
components.chinese: 我認為，成為一名職業運動員既刺激、也很有回報，但同時也非常艱苦。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: I think being a professional athlete is exciting and rewarding, but also
incredibly demanding.
[我認為，成為一名職業運動員既刺激、也很有回報，但同時也非常艱苦。]

components.english: On the surface, it may look glamorous, because people see medals, fame, travel, and big competitions. However, that is only the tip of the iceberg. Behind the scenes, athletes usually live highly disciplined lives filled with early mornings, intense training, strict diets, and constant pressure to perform. In many ways, their body is both their instrument and their livelihood, so they have to take care of it with enormous discipline.
components.chinese: 表面上看，這種生活似乎很光鮮，因為人們看到的是獎牌、名氣、旅行，以及大型比賽。不過，那其實只是不過是冰山一角。幕後的生活通常充滿了清晨訓練、高強度操練、嚴格飲食，以及持續不斷的表現壓力。某程度上，他們的身體既是工具，也是飯碗，所以他們必須以極大的自律去照顧它。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: On the surface, it may look glamorous, because people see
medals, fame, travel, and big competitions. However, that is only the tip of the
iceberg. Behind the scenes, athletes usually live highly disciplined lives filled
with early mornings, intense training, strict diets, and constant pressure to
perform. In many ways, their body is both their instrument and their
livelihood, so they have to take care of it with enormous discipline.
[表面上看，這種生活似乎很光鮮，因為人們看到的是獎牌、名氣、旅行，以及大型比賽。不
過，那其實只是不過是冰山一角。幕後的生活通常充滿了清晨訓練、高強度操練、嚴格飲食
，以及持續不斷的表現壓力。某程度上，他們的身體既是工具，也是飯碗，所以他們必須以
極大的自律去照顧它。]

components.english: For example, a professional swimmer, runner, or football player may train for hours every day, stick to a strict routine, and still have to perform well when it matters most. Even when they are tired, sore, or mentally drained, they often have to keep going.
components.chinese: 例如，一名職業游泳選手、跑手，或者足球員，可能每天都要訓練好幾個小時，遵守非常嚴格的生活規律，而且到了最關鍵的時刻，仍然必須交出好表現。即使他們已經很累、身體痠痛，或者精神上被耗得很厲害，也往往還是要繼續撐下去。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, a professional swimmer, runner, or football player may
train for hours every day, stick to a strict routine, and still have to perform well
when it matters most. Even when they are tired, sore, or mentally drained, they
often have to keep going.
[例如，一名職業游泳選手、跑手，或者足球員，可能每天都要訓練好幾個小時，遵守非常嚴
格的生活規律，而且到了最關鍵的時刻，仍然必須交出好表現。即使他們已經很累、身體痠
痛，或者精神上被耗得很厲害，也往往還是要繼續撐下去。]

components.english: So, to me, being a professional athlete may look thrilling from the outside, but in reality it is a life built on sacrifice, discipline, and resilience.
components.chinese: 所以，在我看來，成為職業運動員從外面看也許很精彩，但實際上，那是一種建立在犧牲、自律和韌性之上的生活。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, to me, being a professional athlete may look thrilling from
the outside, but in reality it is a life built on sacrifice, discipline, and resilience.
[所以，在我看來，成為職業運動員從外面看也許很精彩，但實際上，那是一種建立在犧牲、
自律和韌性之上的生活。]

components.english: Another important side of it is uncertainty, because an athlete’s career can be intense but also fragile.
components.chinese: 另一個很重要的層面是，它同時充滿不確定性，因為運動員的職業生涯雖然強烈而耀眼，卻也相當脆弱。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: Another important side of it is uncertainty, because an athlete’s career
can be intense but also fragile.
[另一個很重要的層面是，它同時充滿不確定性，因為運動員的職業生涯雖然強烈而耀眼，
卻也相當脆弱。]

components.english: A professional athlete often walks a fine line between success and setback. One injury, one poor season, or one missed chance can change everything. On top of that, many athletes have to deal with public expectations, competition from younger players, and the knowledge that their career may not last very long. It is not always a bed of roses. In fact, it can be mentally exhausting as well as physically punishing.
components.chinese: 一名職業運動員很多時候都像是在成功和挫折之間走鋼線。一次受傷、一個表現不佳的賽季，或者一次錯失的機會，都可能改變一切。除此之外，很多運動員還要面對公眾期望、年輕選手帶來的競爭，以及對自己職業生涯未必能維持很長的清楚認知。這絕對不是一條鋪滿玫瑰花瓣的路。事實上，它在心理上和身體上都可能同樣折磨人。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: A professional athlete often walks a fine line between success
and setback. One injury, one poor season, or one missed chance can change
everything. On top of that, many athletes have to deal with public expectations,
competition from younger players, and the knowledge that their career may not
last very long. It is not always a bed of roses. In fact, it can be mentally
exhausting as well as physically punishing.
[一名職業運動員很多時候都像是在成功和挫折之間走鋼線。一次受傷、一個表現不佳的賽
季，或者一次錯失的機會，都可能改變一切。除此之外，很多運動員還要面對公眾期望、年
輕選手帶來的競爭，以及對自己職業生涯未必能維持很長的清楚認知。這絕對不是一條鋪
滿玫瑰花瓣的路。事實上，它在心理上和身體上都可能同樣折磨人。]

components.english: For instance, an athlete may spend years preparing for one major event and give up holidays, comfort, and even time with family just to be in peak condition. If they succeed, the reward can be extraordinary. But if things go wrong, the disappointment can hit like a ton of bricks.
components.chinese: 例如，一名運動員可能會為了一場重要賽事準備好幾年，放棄假期、舒適生活，甚至和家人相處的時間，只為了讓自己處於最佳狀態。如果他成功了，那份回報當然可能非常巨大；但如果事情出了差錯，那種失望也可能沉重得像一整噸磚頭壓下來一樣。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, an athlete may spend years preparing for one major
event and give up holidays, comfort, and even time with family just to be in
peak condition. If they succeed, the reward can be extraordinary. But if things
go wrong, the disappointment can hit like a ton of bricks.
[例如，一名運動員可能會為了一場重要賽事準備好幾年，放棄假期、舒適生活，甚至和家
人相處的時間，只為了讓自己處於最佳狀態。如果他成功了，那份回報當然可能非常巨大；
但如果事情出了差錯，那種失望也可能沉重得像一整噸磚頭壓下來一樣。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say being a professional athlete is a life of high highs and hard lows. It can be deeply fulfilling for people who truly love their sport, but it also demands far more than most people realise.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說成為職業運動員是一種高峰很高、低谷也很深的人生。對那些真正熱愛自己運動項目的人來說，它可以帶來很深的滿足感；不過，它所要求付出的東西，也遠比大多數人想像中更多。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say being a professional athlete is a life of high
highs and hard lows. It can be deeply fulfilling for people who truly love their
sport, but it also demands far more than most people realise.
[總結來說，我會說成為職業運動員是一種高峰很高、低谷也很深的人生。對那些真正熱愛
自己運動項目的人來說，它可以帶來很深的滿足感；不過，它所要求付出的東西，也遠比大
多數人想像中更多。]

question.english: Can you compare some exciting activities people do now with activities people did 20 years ago?
question.chinese: 你可以比較一下現在人們做的一些刺激活動，和二十年前人們做的活動嗎？

source_lines.text: Topic: Can you compare some exciting activities people do now with activities
people did 20 years ago?
你可以比較一下現在人們做的一些刺激活動，和二十年前人們做的活動
嗎？

components.english: Yes, I’d say exciting activities today are often more technology-driven, more fast-paced, and sometimes more visually spectacular than the activities people did 20 years ago.
components.chinese: 可以，我會說，現在令人覺得刺激的活動，通常比二十年前更依賴科技、節奏更快，而且有時在視覺效果上也更誇張。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: Yes, I’d say exciting activities today are often more technology-driven,
more fast-paced, and sometimes more visually spectacular than the activities
people did 20 years ago.
[可以，我會說，現在令人覺得刺激的活動，通常比二十年前更依賴科技、節奏更快，而且有
時在視覺效果上也更誇張。]

components.english: These days, a lot of excitement is tied to technology, novelty, and shareability. People might go to VR gaming centres, try immersive escape rooms, take part in extreme sports, or travel mainly for eye-catching experiences that look good online. In a sense, excitement today is often wrapped up with speed, stimulation, and the urge to capture the moment. Twenty years ago, by contrast, exciting activities were usually simpler and less screen-based. People still had fun, of course, but the thrill often came more from the activity itself than from the whole package around it.
components.chinese: 現在，很多「刺激感」都和科技、新鮮感，以及是否值得分享有關。人們可能會去玩 VR 遊戲、參加沉浸式密室逃脫、體驗極限運動，或者特意去旅行，只為了一些很上鏡、很適合放到網上的體驗。某程度上，今天的刺激感常常和速度、感官刺激，以及想把那一刻記錄下來的衝動綁在一起。相比之下，二十年前那些令人興奮的活動通常較簡單，也較少依賴螢幕。當然，那時的人同樣玩得開心，只不過那種興奮更多來自活動本身，而不是它周邊整個包裝。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: These days, a lot of excitement is tied to technology, novelty, and
shareability. People might go to VR gaming centres, try immersive escape
rooms, take part in extreme sports, or travel mainly for eye-catching experiences
that look good online. In a sense, excitement today is often wrapped up with
speed, stimulation, and the urge to capture the moment. Twenty years ago, by
contrast, exciting activities were usually simpler and less screen-based. People
still had fun, of course, but the thrill often came more from the activity itself
than from the whole package around it.
[現在，很多「刺激感」都和科技、新鮮感，以及是否值得分享有關。人們可能會去玩 VR 遊
戲、參加沉浸式密室逃脫、體驗極限運動，或者特意去旅行，只為了一些很上鏡、很適合放
到網上的體驗。某程度上，今天的刺激感常常和速度、感官刺激，以及想把那一刻記錄下來
的衝動綁在一起。相比之下，二十年前那些令人興奮的活動通常較簡單，也較少依賴螢幕。
當然，那時的人同樣玩得開心，只不過那種興奮更多來自活動本身，而不是它周邊整個包

components.english: For example, now some young people may go indoor skydiving, try ziplining, or spend hours on interactive games that feel almost cinematic. Twenty years ago, people were more likely to get their kicks from things like going to an amusement park, playing team sports, visiting arcades, or simply hanging out with friends outdoors. Both can be exciting, but the flavour of the excitement feels different.
components.chinese: 例如，現在有些年輕人可能會去玩室內跳傘、滑索，或者花很多時間玩那些幾乎像電影一樣的互動遊戲。二十年前，人們則更可能從去遊樂場、打團體運動、逛電子遊戲機中心，或者單純和朋友在戶外玩樂之中找到刺激感。兩者都可以很令人興奮，只不過那種興奮的味道不太一樣。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, now some young people may go indoor skydiving, try
ziplining, or spend hours on interactive games that feel almost cinematic.
Twenty years ago, people were more likely to get their kicks from things like
going to an amusement park, playing team sports, visiting arcades, or simply
hanging out with friends outdoors. Both can be exciting, but the flavour of the
excitement feels different.
[例如，現在有些年輕人可能會去玩室內跳傘、滑索，或者花很多時間玩那些幾乎像電影一
樣的互動遊戲。二十年前，人們則更可能從去遊樂場、打團體運動、逛電子遊戲機中心，或
者單純和朋友在戶外玩樂之中找到刺激感。兩者都可以很令人興奮，只不過那種興奮的味
道不太一樣。]

components.english: So, one clear difference is that exciting activities today often feel more high-tech and high-intensity, whereas those in the past were often more straightforward and less mediated by technology.
components.chinese: 所以，其中一個很明顯的分別就是，今天的刺激活動通常更高科技、也更高強度；而過去的活動則往往較直接，也較少被科技介入。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, one clear difference is that exciting activities today often feel
more high-tech and high-intensity, whereas those in the past were often more
straightforward and less mediated by technology.
[所以，其中一個很明顯的分別就是，今天的刺激活動通常更高科技、也更高強度；而過去
的活動則往往較直接，也較少被科技介入。]

components.english: That said, I also think exciting activities 20 years ago were often more social and more spontaneous, whereas many activities now can feel a bit more individualised or performative.
components.chinese: 不過，我也認為，二十年前那些刺激活動往往更講求社交，也更自然隨興；而現在很多活動則可能顯得更個人化，甚至有點表演性。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I also think exciting activities 20 years ago were often more
social and more spontaneous, whereas many activities now can feel a bit more
individualised or performative.
[不過，我也認為，二十年前那些刺激活動往往更講求社交，也更自然隨興；而現在很多活
動則可能顯得更個人化，甚至有點表演性。]

components.english: In the past, people usually had fewer options, so they made more out of what they had. Excitement often came from being physically present with others, whether that meant playing football in a park, going karaoke with friends, or staying out late talking and laughing. Now, people still do social activities, of course, but some excitement is filtered through phones and social media. It is not just about enjoying the moment; sometimes it is also about posting it, proving it, and putting it on display. That can make modern fun feel bigger on the surface, but not always deeper underneath.
components.chinese: 過去，人們通常選擇較少，所以反而更懂得把手上的活動玩出味道來。那種興奮感很多時來自和別人真正在同一個空間裡，例如在公園踢足球、和朋友去唱 K，或者晚上一起聊天、大笑到很晚。現在的人當然也會做社交活動，不過有些刺激感會先經過手機和社交媒體。人們不再只是享受那一刻；有時也像是在發佈它、證明它，甚至展示它。這會令現代的快樂表面上看起來更大，但未必一定更深。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: In the past, people usually had fewer options, so they made
more out of what they had. Excitement often came from being physically
present with others, whether that meant playing football in a park, going
karaoke with friends, or staying out late talking and laughing. Now, people still
do social activities, of course, but some excitement is filtered through phones
and social media. It is not just about enjoying the moment; sometimes it is also
about posting it, proving it, and putting it on display. That can make modern
fun feel bigger on the surface, but not always deeper underneath.
[過去，人們通常選擇較少，所以反而更懂得把手上的活動玩出味道來。那種興奮感很多時
來自和別人真正在同一個空間裡，例如在公園踢足球、和朋友去唱 K，或者晚上一起聊天、
大笑到很晚。現在的人當然也會做社交活動，不過有些刺激感會先經過手機和社交媒體。人
們不再只是享受那一刻；有時也像是在發佈它、證明它，甚至展示它。這會令現代的快樂表
面上看起來更大，但未必一定更深。]

components.english: For instance, nowadays someone might go hiking partly for the scenery and partly for the photos, whereas 20 years ago the same trip may simply have been about the fresh air, the company, and the day out itself. In the same way, a night out now may revolve around trendy venues and online posts, while in the past it might just have meant a simple meal, a film, and good conversation.
components.chinese: 例如，現在有人去行山，可能一部分是為了風景，另一部分也是為了拍照；但在二十年前，同樣的一趟行山，可能就只是單純為了新鮮空氣、同行的人，以及那一天本身。同樣地，現在的一次夜生活可能會圍繞著熱門地方和網上發文，而過去則可能只是簡單地吃頓飯、看場電影，然後好好聊天。

source_lines.text: 7. Example 2: For instance, nowadays someone might go hiking partly for the
scenery and partly for the photos, whereas 20 years ago the same trip may
simply have been about the fresh air, the company, and the day out itself. In the
same way, a night out now may revolve around trendy venues and online posts,
while in the past it might just have meant a simple meal, a film, and good
conversation.
[例如，現在有人去行山，可能一部分是為了風景，另一部分也是為了拍照；但在二十年前，
同樣的一趟行山，可能就只是單純為了新鮮空氣、同行的人，以及那一天本身。同樣地，現
在的一次夜生活可能會圍繞著熱門地方和網上發文，而過去則可能只是簡單地吃頓飯、看
場電影，然後好好聊天。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say exciting activities now tend to be more high-tech, more varied, and sometimes more intense, while activities 20 years ago were often simpler, more face-to-face, and less filtered. Neither is automatically better. Modern activities may offer a bigger buzz, but older ones often had a kind of warmth and spontaneity that is hard to beat.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說，現在的刺激活動通常更多高科技元素、更多樣化，而且有時更強烈；而二十年前的活動則往往較簡單、更著重面對面互動，也較少經過包裝。兩者並沒有哪一種一定更好。現代活動也許能帶來更大的刺激感，但過去那些活動往往有一種很難取代的溫度和自然感。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say exciting activities now tend to be more
high-tech, more varied, and sometimes more intense, while activities 20 years
ago were often simpler, more face-to-face, and less filtered. Neither is
automatically better. Modern activities may offer a bigger buzz, but older ones
often had a kind of warmth and spontaneity that is hard to beat.
[總結來說，我會說，現在的刺激活動通常更多高科技元素、更多樣化，而且有時更強烈；而
二十年前的活動則往往較簡單、更著重面對面互動，也較少經過包裝。兩者並沒有哪一種一
定更好。現代活動也許能帶來更大的刺激感，但過去那些活動往往有一種很難取代的溫度
和自然感。]

question.english: Why do some people enjoy doing dangerous sports?
question.chinese: 為甚麼有些人喜歡進行危險運動？

source_lines.text: Topic: Why do some people enjoy doing dangerous sports?
[主題：為甚麼有些人喜歡進行危險運動？]

components.english: I think one major reason is that dangerous sports give some people an intense thrill that ordinary life simply cannot match.
components.chinese: 我認為，其中一個主要原因是，危險運動能帶來一種日常生活根本難以比擬的強烈刺激感。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: I think one major reason is that dangerous sports give some people an
intense thrill that ordinary life simply cannot match.
[我認為，其中一個主要原因是，危險運動能帶來一種日常生活根本難以比擬的強烈刺激

components.english: For some people, activities like rock climbing, skydiving, or surfing huge waves make them feel vividly alive. There is adrenaline, there is uncertainty, and there is that sharp edge of risk that makes every second feel more real. In everyday life, many routines can feel safe but a little flat. By contrast, dangerous sports jolt people awake. In that sense, it is not only about danger itself; it is about the rush, the focus, and the feeling that life is suddenly burning a little brighter.
components.chinese: 對某些人來說，像攀石、跳傘，或者衝大浪這類活動，會令他們感到自己活得特別真切。那裡面有腎上腺素、有不確定性，也有那種風險擦身而過的銳利感，令每一秒都變得格外真實。在日常生活裡，很多節奏雖然安全，卻可能有點平淡。相比之下，危險運動會一下子把人震醒。從這個角度來說，人們追求的未必只是危險本身，而是那股衝擊感、那種高度專注，以及人生突然變得更鮮明的感覺。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: For some people, activities like rock climbing, skydiving, or
surfing huge waves make them feel vividly alive. There is adrenaline, there is
uncertainty, and there is that sharp edge of risk that makes every second feel
more real. In everyday life, many routines can feel safe but a little flat. By
contrast, dangerous sports jolt people awake. In that sense, it is not only about
danger itself; it is about the rush, the focus, and the feeling that life is suddenly
burning a little brighter.
[對某些人來說，像攀石、跳傘，或者衝大浪這類活動，會令他們感到自己活得特別真切。那
裡面有腎上腺素、有不確定性，也有那種風險擦身而過的銳利感，令每一秒都變得格外真
實。在日常生活裡，很多節奏雖然安全，卻可能有點平淡。相比之下，危險運動會一下子把
人震醒。從這個角度來說，人們追求的未必只是危險本身，而是那股衝擊感、那種高度專注
，以及人生突然變得更鮮明的感覺。]

components.english: For example, someone who goes bungee jumping may say that the few seconds before the jump feel terrifying, but also strangely exhilarating. Once they have done it, they often talk about the freedom of the experience and the incredible high that comes afterwards.
components.chinese: 例如，一個去笨豬跳的人可能會說，跳下去前那幾秒既可怕，又奇妙地令人興奮。而當他完成之後，往往又會提到那種強烈的自由感，以及之後湧上來的巨大快感。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, someone who goes bungee jumping may say that the
few seconds before the jump feel terrifying, but also strangely exhilarating.
Once they have done it, they often talk about the freedom of the experience and
the incredible high that comes afterwards.
[例如，一個去笨豬跳的人可能會說，跳下去前那幾秒既可怕，又奇妙地令人興奮。而當他
完成之後，往往又會提到那種強烈的自由感，以及之後湧上來的巨大快感。]

components.english: So, in many cases, people enjoy dangerous sports because those activities offer excitement, intensity, and a rare feeling of being fully present in the moment.
components.chinese: 所以，在很多情況下，人們喜歡危險運動，是因為這些活動能帶來刺激、強烈感受，以及一種很少見的、完全活在當下的感覺。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, in many cases, people enjoy dangerous sports because those
activities offer excitement, intensity, and a rare feeling of being fully present in
the moment.
[所以，在很多情況下，人們喜歡危險運動，是因為這些活動能帶來刺激、強烈感受，以及一
種很少見的、完全活在當下的感覺。]

components.english: Another reason is that dangerous sports allow people to test their limits and prove something to themselves.
components.chinese: 另一個原因是，危險運動能讓人測試自己的極限，並向自己證明一些事情。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: Another reason is that dangerous sports allow people to test their
limits and prove something to themselves.
[另一個原因是，危險運動能讓人測試自己的極限，並向自己證明一些事情。]

components.english: I think many people are drawn to these sports because they are not just chasing a thrill; they are also chasing growth. When someone takes on a real physical or mental challenge, they often discover how much fear they can manage, how calm they can stay under pressure, and how far they can push themselves without falling apart. It becomes a battle not only with the mountain, the wave, or the height, but also with the voice inside their head that says, “You can’t do this.” That is probably why dangerous sports can feel like a rite of passage rather than just a hobby.
components.chinese: 我認為，很多人被這類運動吸引，不只是因為他們追求刺激，同時也是因為他們在追求成長。當一個人真正面對身體或心理上的挑戰時，他往往會發現自己究竟能承受多少恐懼、能在壓力下保持多冷靜，以及能把自己推到多遠而不至於崩潰。那時候，那不只是和高山、巨浪，或者高度本身在對抗，同時也是在和自己腦海裡那把說「你做不到」的聲音較量。這大概也是為甚麼危險運動有時給人的感覺更像一場成長儀式，而不只是一種興趣。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: I think many people are drawn to these sports because they are
not just chasing a thrill; they are also chasing growth. When someone takes on a
real physical or mental challenge, they often discover how much fear they can
manage, how calm they can stay under pressure, and how far they can push
themselves without falling apart. It becomes a battle not only with the
mountain, the wave, or the height, but also with the voice inside their head that
says, “You can’t do this.” That is probably why dangerous sports can feel like a
rite of passage rather than just a hobby.
[我認為，很多人被這類運動吸引，不只是因為他們追求刺激，同時也是因為他們在追求成
長。當一個人真正面對身體或心理上的挑戰時，他往往會發現自己究竟能承受多少恐懼、能
在壓力下保持多冷靜，以及能把自己推到多遠而不至於崩潰。那時候，那不只是和高山、巨
浪，或者高度本身在對抗，同時也是在和自己腦海裡那把說「你做不到」的聲音較量。這大概
也是為甚麼危險運動有時給人的感覺更像一場成長儀式，而不只是一種興趣。]

components.english: For instance, a person who learns to climb difficult routes may begin with fear and hesitation, but over time they build technique, confidence, and self-control. Reaching the top then feels like much more than a physical success; it feels like they have climbed over a piece of themselves.
components.chinese: 例如，一個學習攀爬高難度路線的人，最初可能充滿恐懼和猶豫，但隨著時間過去，他會慢慢建立技巧、自信，以及自我控制能力。當他最後到達頂端時，那種成就感就不再只是身體上的成功；它更像是他跨越了自己內心的一部分。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, a person who learns to climb difficult routes may
begin with fear and hesitation, but over time they build technique, confidence,
and self-control. Reaching the top then feels like much more than a physical
success; it feels like they have climbed over a piece of themselves.
[例如，一個學習攀爬高難度路線的人，最初可能充滿恐懼和猶豫，但隨著時間過去，他會
慢慢建立技巧、自信，以及自我控制能力。當他最後到達頂端時，那種成就感就不再只是身
體上的成功；它更像是他跨越了自己內心的一部分。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say some people enjoy dangerous sports because they offer both thrill and transformation. Of course, it is not everyone’s cup of tea, and it can become reckless if people ignore safety. But when it is done responsibly, what looks like danger from the outside may feel, to them, like freedom, challenge, and a deeper way of feeling alive.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說有些人喜歡危險運動，是因為它同時帶來刺激感和轉變感。當然，這不是每個人都會喜歡的東西，而且如果忽視安全，它也可能變成魯莽行為。不過，只要做得負責任，從外面看起來像危險的事，對他們而言，卻可能是一種自由、一種挑戰，以及一種更深刻感受自己活著的方式。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say some people enjoy dangerous sports because
they offer both thrill and transformation. Of course, it is not everyone’s cup of
tea, and it can become reckless if people ignore safety. But when it is done
responsibly, what looks like danger from the outside may feel, to them, like
freedom, challenge, and a deeper way of feeling alive.
[總結來說，我會說有些人喜歡危險運動，是因為它同時帶來刺激感和轉變感。當然，這不
是每個人都會喜歡的東西，而且如果忽視安全，它也可能變成魯莽行為。不過，只要做得負
責任，從外面看起來像危險的事，對他們而言，卻可能是一種自由、一種挑戰，以及一種更
深刻感受自己活著的方式。]

question.english: Do you think some dangerous activities should be banned?
question.chinese: 你認為有些危險活動應該被禁止嗎？

source_lines.text: Topic: Do you think some dangerous activities should be banned?
你認為有些危險活動應該被禁止嗎？

components.english: Yes, I do think some dangerous activities should be banned, especially when the risk is extreme and innocent other people can be harmed as well.
components.chinese: 是的，我的確認為有些危險活動應該被禁止，尤其是當風險高到很極端，而且連無辜的其他人也可能受到傷害的時候。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: Yes, I do think some dangerous activities should be banned, especially
when the risk is extreme and innocent other people can be harmed as well.
[是的，我的確認為有些危險活動應該被禁止，尤其是當風險高到很極端，而且連無辜的其
他人也可能受到傷害的時候。]

components.english: For me, the key point is that freedom should not come at any price. If an activity is so dangerous that one reckless decision can cost lives, then I think the government has every right to draw the line. At that stage, it is no longer just about thrill-seeking or personal choice; it becomes a matter of public safety. Some risks are simply too high to roll the dice on.
components.chinese: 對我來說，關鍵在於自由不應該是不惜任何代價的。如果一種活動危險到只要一個魯莽決定就可能鬧出人命，那我認為政府完全有權劃出界線。到了那個地步，事情就不再只是追求刺激或個人選擇那麼簡單，而是關乎公共安全。有些風險實在高得不能拿來碰運氣。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: For me, the key point is that freedom should not come at any
price. If an activity is so dangerous that one reckless decision can cost lives, then
I think the government has every right to draw the line. At that stage, it is no
longer just about thrill-seeking or personal choice; it becomes a matter of public
safety. Some risks are simply too high to roll the dice on.
[對我來說，關鍵在於自由不應該是不惜任何代價的。如果一種活動危險到只要一個魯莽決
定就可能鬧出人命，那我認為政府完全有權劃出界線。到了那個地步，事情就不再只是追求
刺激或個人選擇那麼簡單，而是關乎公共安全。有些風險實在高得不能拿來碰運氣。]

components.english: For example, illegal street racing or reckless stunts in crowded public places should definitely be banned. They may look exciting for a few minutes, but if something goes wrong, it is not only the people taking part who suffer; bystanders can end up paying the price too.
components.chinese: 例如，非法賽車，或者在擠迫公共地方進行魯莽特技表演，我覺得就肯定應該禁止。那些活動也許看起來刺激幾分鐘，但一旦出事，受害的不只是參與者，連旁邊無辜的人也可能要承受後果。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, illegal street racing or reckless stunts in crowded
public places should definitely be banned. They may look exciting for a few
minutes, but if something goes wrong, it is not only the people taking part who
suffer; bystanders can end up paying the price too.
[例如，非法賽車，或者在擠迫公共地方進行魯莽特技表演，我覺得就肯定應該禁止。那些
活動也許看起來刺激幾分鐘，但一旦出事，受害的不只是參與者，連旁邊無辜的人也可能要
承受後果。]

components.english: So yes, when an activity poses a serious threat to life and public safety, banning it is both reasonable and necessary.
components.chinese: 所以，是的，當一種活動對生命和公共安全構成嚴重威脅時，把它禁止是合理而且有必要的。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So yes, when an activity poses a serious threat to life and public
safety, banning it is both reasonable and necessary.
[所以，是的，當一種活動對生命和公共安全構成嚴重威脅時，把它禁止是合理而且有必要

components.english: That said, I do not think every dangerous activity should be banned outright, because some risks can be managed rather than erased.
components.chinese: 不過，我也不認為每一種危險活動都應該一刀切地禁止，因為有些風險其實是可以被管理，而不是只能被徹底消除。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I do not think every dangerous activity should be banned
outright, because some risks can be managed rather than erased.
[不過，我也不認為每一種危險活動都應該一刀切地禁止，因為有些風險其實是可以被管理
，而不是只能被徹底消除。]

components.english: Some activities are risky by nature, such as rock climbing, scuba diving, or skydiving, but people usually choose them knowingly and prepare for them properly. In cases like that, strict rules, proper training, and good safety equipment make more sense than a blanket ban. Otherwise, we would end up wrapping people in cotton wool and taking all adventure out of life. The real issue, in my view, is not danger alone, but uncontrolled danger.
components.chinese: 有些活動天生就帶有風險，例如攀石、潛水，或者跳傘，但人們通常是在清楚知道風險的情況下才選擇參加，並且會作好準備。在這種情況下，嚴格規則、足夠訓練，以及完善安全裝備，往往比全面禁止更合理。否則，我們就好像把所有人都包在棉花裡一樣，連生活中的冒險精神也一併拿走了。在我看來，真正的問題不只是危險本身，而是失控的危險。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: Some activities are risky by nature, such as rock climbing,
scuba diving, or skydiving, but people usually choose them knowingly and
prepare for them properly. In cases like that, strict rules, proper training, and
good safety equipment make more sense than a blanket ban. Otherwise, we
would end up wrapping people in cotton wool and taking all adventure out of
life. The real issue, in my view, is not danger alone, but uncontrolled danger.
[有些活動天生就帶有風險，例如攀石、潛水，或者跳傘，但人們通常是在清楚知道風險的
情況下才選擇參加，並且會作好準備。在這種情況下，嚴格規則、足夠訓練，以及完善安全
裝備，往往比全面禁止更合理。否則，我們就好像把所有人都包在棉花裡一樣，連生活中的
冒險精神也一併拿走了。在我看來，真正的問題不只是危險本身，而是失控的危險。]

components.english: For instance, mountain climbing can be dangerous, but that does not mean it should disappear completely. If climbers are well-trained, follow safety procedures, and understand what they are getting into, then it can remain a challenging but acceptable activity.
components.chinese: 例如，登山的確可能很危險，但那並不代表它應該完全消失。如果登山者受過足夠訓練、遵守安全程序，而且清楚知道自己面對的是甚麼，那麼這仍然可以是一種有挑戰性但可以接受的活動。

source_lines.text: 7. Example 2: For instance, mountain climbing can be dangerous, but that does
not mean it should disappear completely. If climbers are well-trained, follow
safety procedures, and understand what they are getting into, then it can
remain a challenging but acceptable activity.
[例如，登山的確可能很危險，但那並不代表它應該完全消失。如果登山者受過足夠訓練、
遵守安全程序，而且清楚知道自己面對的是甚麼，那麼這仍然可以是一種有挑戰性但可以
接受的活動。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say the most dangerous activities should be banned when they put unwilling others in harm’s way, but risky activities chosen responsibly should usually be regulated rather than outlawed.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說，當某些危險活動會把不情願的其他人也拖進危險之中時，它們就應該被禁止；但如果是人們在負責任的情況下自願參與的高風險活動，那通常更適合被規管，而不是直接取締。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say the most dangerous activities should be banned
when they put unwilling others in harm’s way, but risky activities chosen
responsibly should usually be regulated rather than outlawed.
[總結來說，我會說，當某些危險活動會把不情願的其他人也拖進危險之中時，它們就應該
被禁止；但如果是人們在負責任的情況下自願參與的高風險活動，那通常更適合被規管，而
不是直接取締。]

question.english: Should people try doing new things?
question.chinese: 人們應該嘗試做新事物嗎？

source_lines.text: Topic: Should people try doing new things?
人們應該嘗試做新事物嗎？

components.english: Yes, I generally think they should, because trying new things helps people grow, broaden their perspective, and keep life from going stale.
components.chinese: 是的，一般來說，我認為人們應該這樣做，因為嘗試新事物能幫助人成長、開闊眼界，也能避免生活變得乏味而停滯。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: Yes, I generally think they should, because trying new things helps
people grow, broaden their perspective, and keep life from going stale.
[是的，一般來說，我認為人們應該這樣做，因為嘗試新事物能幫助人成長、開闊眼界，也能
避免生活變得乏味而停滯。]

components.english: The main reason is that people can easily get stuck in a rut if they only do what feels familiar. New experiences often push people out of their comfort zone, and that is usually where learning begins. Whether it is picking up a new skill, visiting a new place, or speaking to different kinds of people, doing something unfamiliar can shake people awake a little. In that sense, new experiences are like fresh air: they clear the mind and remind people that life can be bigger than their usual routine.
components.chinese: 主要原因是，如果人們永遠只做自己熟悉的事情，就很容易陷入一種一成不變的慣性。新的經歷往往會把人推出舒適圈，而學習通常正是從那裡開始。無論是學一項新技能、去一個新地方，還是和不同類型的人交流，做一些不熟悉的事都能讓人稍微清醒過來。從這個角度來看，新體驗就像新鮮空氣一樣：它能讓頭腦變得更開闊，也提醒人生活其實可以比平日的固定節奏更廣闊。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The main reason is that people can easily get stuck in a rut if
they only do what feels familiar. New experiences often push people out of their
comfort zone, and that is usually where learning begins. Whether it is picking
up a new skill, visiting a new place, or speaking to different kinds of people,
doing something unfamiliar can shake people awake a little. In that sense, new
experiences are like fresh air: they clear the mind and remind people that life
can be bigger than their usual routine.
[主要原因是，如果人們永遠只做自己熟悉的事情，就很容易陷入一種一成不變的慣性。新
的經歷往往會把人推出舒適圈，而學習通常正是從那裡開始。無論是學一項新技能、去一個
新地方，還是和不同類型的人交流，做一些不熟悉的事都能讓人稍微清醒過來。從這個角度
來看，新體驗就像新鮮空氣一樣：它能讓頭腦變得更開闊，也提醒人生活其實可以比平日的
固定節奏更廣闊。]

components.english: For example, someone who decides to learn cooking, join a sports club, or travel somewhere they have never been may discover abilities or interests they did not even know they had. A person who always plays it safe may feel comfortable, but they may also miss out on experiences that could change them for the better.
components.chinese: 例如，一個決定去學煮食、加入運動會，或者去自己從未到過的地方旅行的人，可能會發現一些自己原本都不知道存在的能力或興趣。總是凡事求穩的人或許會覺得舒服，但同時也可能錯過一些能讓自己變得更好的經歷。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, someone who decides to learn cooking, join a sports
club, or travel somewhere they have never been may discover abilities or
interests they did not even know they had. A person who always plays it safe
may feel comfortable, but they may also miss out on experiences that could
change them for the better.
[例如，一個決定去學煮食、加入運動會，或者去自己從未到過的地方旅行的人，可能會發
現一些自己原本都不知道存在的能力或興趣。總是凡事求穩的人或許會覺得舒服，但同時
也可能錯過一些能讓自己變得更好的經歷。]

components.english: So yes, I would say people should try new things, because that is often how they learn more about the world and about themselves.
components.chinese: 所以，是的，我會說人們應該嘗試新事物，因為那往往正是他們更了解世界、也更了解自己的方式。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So yes, I would say people should try new things, because that is
often how they learn more about the world and about themselves.
[所以，是的，我會說人們應該嘗試新事物，因為那往往正是他們更了解世界、也更了解自
己的方式。]

components.english: That said, I do not think people should try new things blindly or just for the sake of novelty.
components.chinese: 不過，我也不認為人們應該盲目地嘗試新事物，或者只是為了追求新鮮感而去做。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I do not think people should try new things blindly or just
for the sake of novelty.
[不過，我也不認為人們應該盲目地嘗試新事物，或者只是為了追求新鮮感而去做。]

components.english: To be honest, not every new idea is a good one, and not every risk is worth taking. Sometimes people can romanticise change, as if anything unfamiliar must automatically be exciting or meaningful. But good judgment still matters. It is one thing to stretch yourself; it is another to throw yourself in at the deep end without thinking things through. In other words, trying new things is valuable when it helps people grow, not when it simply creates unnecessary chaos.
components.chinese: 老實說，並不是每一個新主意都是好主意，也不是每一種風險都值得承擔。有時人們會把改變想像得太美，好像任何不熟悉的東西都一定自動等於刺激或有意義似的。但判斷力仍然很重要。讓自己被拉伸是一回事；沒有想清楚就把自己一下子丟進深水區，又是另一回事。換句話說，當嘗試新事物能幫助人成長時，它才真正有價值；如果只是平白製造混亂，那就未必值得。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: To be honest, not every new idea is a good one, and not every
risk is worth taking. Sometimes people can romanticise change, as if anything
unfamiliar must automatically be exciting or meaningful. But good judgment
still matters. It is one thing to stretch yourself; it is another to throw yourself in
at the deep end without thinking things through. In other words, trying new
things is valuable when it helps people grow, not when it simply creates
unnecessary chaos.
[老實說，並不是每一個新主意都是好主意，也不是每一種風險都值得承擔。有時人們會把
改變想像得太美，好像任何不熟悉的東西都一定自動等於刺激或有意義似的。但判斷力仍
然很重要。讓自己被拉伸是一回事；沒有想清楚就把自己一下子丟進深水區，又是另一回
事。換句話說，當嘗試新事物能幫助人成長時，它才真正有價值；如果只是平白製造混亂，
那就未必值得。]

components.english: For instance, trying a new hobby, applying for a course, or taking on a different kind of project at work can be a healthy challenge. By contrast, making a reckless decision, such as quitting a stable job on impulse with no plan at all, may be new, but that does not make it wise.
components.chinese: 例如，嘗試一個新興趣、報讀一門課程，或者在工作上接下一種不同類型的項目，都可以是一種健康的挑戰。相比之下，作出一個魯莽的決定，例如一時衝動、毫無計劃地辭去穩定工作，雖然也算是「新」，但那並不代表它明智。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, trying a new hobby, applying for a course, or taking
on a different kind of project at work can be a healthy challenge. By contrast,
making a reckless decision, such as quitting a stable job on impulse with no plan
at all, may be new, but that does not make it wise.
[例如，嘗試一個新興趣、報讀一門課程，或者在工作上接下一種不同類型的項目，都可以
是一種健康的挑戰。相比之下，作出一個魯莽的決定，例如一時衝動、毫無計劃地辭去穩定
工作，雖然也算是「新」，但那並不代表它明智。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say people should absolutely try new things, but with a bit of thought and balance. The best new experiences are not the ones that throw people off course in a moment of impulse, but the ones that gradually open the door to new possibilities.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說人們的確應該多嘗試新事物，但同時也要帶著一點思考和分寸。最好的新嘗試，不是讓人一時衝動地偏離軌道，而是讓人慢慢打開新的可能。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say people should absolutely try new things, but with a
bit of thought and balance. The best new experiences are not the ones that throw
people off course in a moment of impulse, but the ones that gradually open the door to
new possibilities.
[總結來說，我會說人們的確應該多嘗試新事物，但同時也要帶著一點思考和分寸。最好的
新嘗試，不是讓人一時衝動地偏離軌道，而是讓人慢慢打開新的可能。]

question.english: What problems can people have when they try new activities for the first time?
question.chinese: 人們第一次嘗試新活動時，可能會遇到甚麼問題？

source_lines.text: What problems can people have when they try new activities for the first time?
主題：人們第一次嘗試新活動時，可能會遇到甚麼問題？

components.english: I think one of the most common problems is that people feel awkward, nervous, or out of their depth when they try a new activity for the first time.
components.chinese: 我認為，最常見的問題之一，就是人們第一次嘗試新活動時，往往會感到尷尬、緊張，或者有一種自己應付不來的感覺。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: I think one of the most common problems is that people feel awkward,
nervous, or out of their depth when they try a new activity for the first time.
[我認為，最常見的問題之一，就是人們第一次嘗試新活動時，往往會感到尷尬、緊張，或者
有一種自己應付不來的感覺。]

components.english: The main reason is that beginners usually do not know the rules, the rhythm, or what is expected of them yet. When people step into unfamiliar territory, they can feel a bit like a fish out of water. They may worry about making mistakes, looking clumsy, or holding other people back. Once that kind of self-consciousness creeps in, even simple tasks can suddenly feel harder than they really are.
components.chinese: 主要原因是，初學者通常還不知道規則、節奏，或者別人對自己的期望是甚麼。當一個人踏進自己不熟悉的領域時，往往就會有一種格格不入的感覺。他們可能會擔心自己犯錯、動作笨拙，或者拖慢別人。一旦這種不自在和自我意識慢慢冒出來，即使原本很簡單的事情，也會突然顯得比實際上更困難。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The main reason is that beginners usually do not know the
rules, the rhythm, or what is expected of them yet. When people step into
unfamiliar territory, they can feel a bit like a fish out of water. They may worry
about making mistakes, looking clumsy, or holding other people back. Once that
kind of self-consciousness creeps in, even simple tasks can suddenly feel harder
than they really are.
[主要原因是，初學者通常還不知道規則、節奏，或者別人對自己的期望是甚麼。當一個人
踏進自己不熟悉的領域時，往往就會有一種格格不入的感覺。他們可能會擔心自己犯錯、動
作笨拙，或者拖慢別人。一旦這種不自在和自我意識慢慢冒出來，即使原本很簡單的事情，
也會突然顯得比實際上更困難。]

components.english: For example, if someone joins a dance class, a yoga lesson, or a sports club for the first time, they may not know the routine, the basic techniques, or even the unspoken etiquette. As a result, they may spend half the time second-guessing themselves instead of actually enjoying the activity.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一個人第一次去上跳舞課、瑜伽課，或者參加運動社團，他可能不清楚流程、基本技巧，甚至連一些不成文的規矩也不知道。結果，他很可能有一半時間都在懷疑自己做得對不對，而不是真正享受那個活動。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if someone joins a dance class, a yoga lesson, or a sports
club for the first time, they may not know the routine, the basic techniques, or
even the unspoken etiquette. As a result, they may spend half the time
second-guessing themselves instead of actually enjoying the activity.
[例如，如果一個人第一次去上跳舞課、瑜伽課，或者參加運動社團，他可能不清楚流程、基
本技巧，甚至連一些不成文的規矩也不知道。結果，他很可能有一半時間都在懷疑自己做得
對不對，而不是真正享受那個活動。]

components.english: So, I would say the first difficulty is often psychological rather than technical, because a lack of confidence can trip people up before the activity itself really does.
components.chinese: 所以，我會說，第一次的困難往往先是心理上的，而不一定只是技術上的，因為很多時候，真正先把人絆住的，不是活動本身，而是信心不足。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, I would say the first difficulty is often psychological rather
than technical, because a lack of confidence can trip people up before the
activity itself really does.
[所以，我會說，第一次的困難往往先是心理上的，而不一定只是技術上的，因為很多時候，
真正先把人絆住的，不是活動本身，而是信心不足。]

components.english: On top of that, people can also run into practical problems, such as not being skilled enough yet, underestimating the effort involved, or expecting themselves to improve too quickly.
components.chinese: 除此之外，人們也可能遇到一些很實際的問題，例如技巧還不夠、低估了活動所需要的投入，或者對自己的進步速度抱有太高期望。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: On top of that, people can also run into practical problems, such as not
being skilled enough yet, underestimating the effort involved, or expecting
themselves to improve too quickly.
[除此之外，人們也可能遇到一些很實際的問題，例如技巧還不夠、低估了活動所需要的投
入，或者對自己的進步速度抱有太高期望。]

components.english: To be honest, many new activities come with a learning curve, and that can be frustrating at first. People sometimes imagine they will pick something up straight away, but real progress is usually slower and messier than that. They may get tired more quickly than expected, struggle with coordination, or feel discouraged when they realise they are not good at it yet. In other words, the problem is not always the activity itself; sometimes it is the gap between expectation and reality.
components.chinese: 老實說，很多新活動本身都有一段學習曲線，而這在一開始往往會令人感到挫敗。有些人會以為自己很快就能上手，但真正的進步通常比想像中更慢，也更混亂。他們可能會比預期中更快覺得疲累、在協調上出問題，或者當他們發現自己還做不好時，就容易感到氣餒。換句話說，問題未必總是在活動本身；有時候，真正讓人受挫的，是期望與現實之間的落差。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: To be honest, many new activities come with a learning curve,
and that can be frustrating at first. People sometimes imagine they will pick
something up straight away, but real progress is usually slower and messier than
that. They may get tired more quickly than expected, struggle with
coordination, or feel discouraged when they realise they are not good at it yet.
In other words, the problem is not always the activity itself; sometimes it is the
gap between expectation and reality.
[老實說，很多新活動本身都有一段學習曲線，而這在一開始往往會令人感到挫敗。有些人
會以為自己很快就能上手，但真正的進步通常比想像中更慢，也更混亂。他們可能會比預期
中更快覺得疲累、在協調上出問題，或者當他們發現自己還做不好時，就容易感到氣餒。換
句話說，問題未必總是在活動本身；有時候，真正讓人受挫的，是期望與現實之間的落差。]

components.english: For instance, if someone starts hiking, swimming, or learning a musical instrument, they may assume it looks easy from the outside. However, once they actually try it, they may realise it takes stamina, patience, and repeated practice. That can be a bit of a wake-up call.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一個人開始行山、學游泳，或者學一種樂器，他可能原本以為那些事情看起來很簡單。不過，當他真的去嘗試時，才會發現那需要體力、耐性，以及反覆練習。那往往會成為一個令人清醒過來的提醒。

source_lines.text: 7. Example 2: For instance, if someone starts hiking, swimming, or learning a
musical instrument, they may assume it looks easy from the outside. However,
once they actually try it, they may realise it takes stamina, patience, and
repeated practice. That can be a bit of a wake-up call.
[例如，如果一個人開始行山、學游泳，或者學一種樂器，他可能原本以為那些事情看起來
很簡單。不過，當他真的去嘗試時，才會發現那需要體力、耐性，以及反覆練習。那往往會成
為一個令人清醒過來的提醒。]

components.english: Overall, I think the biggest problems people face when trying new activities for the first time are nervousness, inexperience, and unrealistic expectations. Still, that is all part of the process, and in many cases, the awkward beginning is simply the price of getting better at something new.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我認為人們第一次嘗試新活動時，最大的問題通常是緊張、不熟練，以及對自己有不切實際的期望。不過，這些其實都是過程的一部分；很多時候，那種一開始的笨拙和不適應，只不過是學會新事物所要付出的入場費。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I think the biggest problems people face when trying
new activities for the first time are nervousness, inexperience, and unrealistic
expectations. Still, that is all part of the process, and in many cases, the
awkward beginning is simply the price of getting better at something new.
[總結來說，我認為人們第一次嘗試新活動時，最大的問題通常是緊張、不熟練，以及對自
己有不切實際的期望。不過，這些其實都是過程的一部分；很多時候，那種一開始的笨拙和
不適應，只不過是學會新事物所要付出的入場費。]

question.english: Do people in your country spend a lot of money on celebrating birthdays?
question.chinese: 你國家的人會花很多錢慶祝生日嗎？

source_lines.text: Do people in your country spend a lot of money on celebrating birthdays?
你國家的人會花很多錢慶祝生日嗎？

components.english: In Hong Kong, I’d say some people do spend quite a lot on birthdays, especially when it is a milestone birthday or a celebration for children, but it really varies from family to family.
components.chinese: 在香港，我會說有些人確實會花不少錢慶祝生日，特別是遇上重要年齡的生日，或者是替小朋友慶祝的時候；不過，這件事其實很視乎不同家庭的習慣。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: In Hong Kong, I’d say some people do spend quite a lot on birthdays,
especially when it is a milestone birthday or a celebration for children, but it
really varies from family to family.
[在香港，我會說有些人確實會花不少錢慶祝生日，特別是遇上重要年齡的生日，或者是替
小朋友慶祝的時候；不過，這件事其實很視乎不同家庭的習慣。]

components.english: The reason is that birthdays are often seen not just as private occasions, but as social ones as well. People may book a restaurant, order a beautiful cake, buy gifts, decorate the place, and invite relatives or close friends. In a city like Hong Kong, where eating out is part and parcel of daily life, it is quite easy for a birthday celebration to grow arms and legs and become a fairly costly event. When people want to make the day feel special, the bill can climb before they know it.
components.chinese: 原因是，生日很多時候不只是被視為私人日子，同時也被看成一種社交場合。人們可能會訂餐廳、買一個漂亮蛋糕、準備禮物、佈置場地，還會邀請親戚或親密朋友一起來。在像香港這樣一個外出用餐本來就很常見的城市裡，一場生日慶祝其實很容易越搞越大，最後變成一個開支不小的活動。當人們想把那一天搞得特別一點時，帳單很多時就在不知不覺間往上升。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The reason is that birthdays are often seen not just as private
occasions, but as social ones as well. People may book a restaurant, order a
beautiful cake, buy gifts, decorate the place, and invite relatives or close friends.
In a city like Hong Kong, where eating out is part and parcel of daily life, it is
quite easy for a birthday celebration to grow arms and legs and become a fairly
costly event. When people want to make the day feel special, the bill can climb
before they know it.
[原因是，生日很多時候不只是被視為私人日子，同時也被看成一種社交場合。人們可能會
訂餐廳、買一個漂亮蛋糕、準備禮物、佈置場地，還會邀請親戚或親密朋友一起來。在像香
港這樣一個外出用餐本來就很常見的城市裡，一場生日慶祝其實很容易越搞越大，最後變
成一個開支不小的活動。當人們想把那一天搞得特別一點時，帳單很多時就在不知不覺間
往上升。]

components.english: For example, if a family celebrates a grandparent’s seventieth birthday or a child’s first birthday, they may go all out by booking a private room in a restaurant, ordering a customised cake, and preparing return gifts or decorations. In that kind of situation, what starts as a simple celebration can quickly snowball into a fairly expensive occasion.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一個家庭要替祖父母慶祝七十大壽，或者替小朋友慶祝一歲生日，他們可能就會相當隆重，例如訂酒樓或餐廳的包廂、訂製蛋糕，甚至準備回禮或場地裝飾。在這種情況下，本來只是簡單慶祝一下的事情，很快就可能越滾越大，變成一個花費相當可觀的場合。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if a family celebrates a grandparent’s seventieth
birthday or a child’s first birthday, they may go all out by booking a private
room in a restaurant, ordering a customised cake, and preparing return gifts or
decorations. In that kind of situation, what starts as a simple celebration can
quickly snowball into a fairly expensive occasion.
[例如，如果一個家庭要替祖父母慶祝七十大壽，或者替小朋友慶祝一歲生日，他們可能就
會相當隆重，例如訂酒樓或餐廳的包廂、訂製蛋糕，甚至準備回禮或場地裝飾。在這種情況
下，本來只是簡單慶祝一下的事情，很快就可能越滾越大，變成一個花費相當可觀的場

components.english: So yes, some people in Hong Kong do spend a lot on birthdays, especially when the celebration carries emotional or family significance.
components.chinese: 所以，是的，香港確實有些人會在生日上花很多錢，特別是當那次慶祝本身帶有很重的情感意義，或者牽涉家庭團聚的時候。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So yes, some people in Hong Kong do spend a lot on birthdays,
especially when the celebration carries emotional or family significance.
[所以，是的，香港確實有些人會在生日上花很多錢，特別是當那次慶祝本身帶有很重的情
感意義，或者牽涉家庭團聚的時候。]

components.english: That said, I do not think everyone splashes out, because many people now prefer simpler and more low-key celebrations.
components.chinese: 不過，我並不認為每個人都會大灑金錢，因為現在也有很多人比較喜歡簡單、低調一點的慶祝方式。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I do not think everyone splashes out, because many people
now prefer simpler and more low-key celebrations.
[不過，我並不認為每個人都會大灑金錢，因為現在也有很多人比較喜歡簡單、低調一點的
慶祝方式。]

components.english: To be honest, Hong Kong is an expensive place to live, so many people think twice before spending heavily on one single day. On top of that, younger people in particular often value the atmosphere more than the price tag. A quiet dinner, a homemade cake, or just spending time with a few close friends may mean more to them than making a big song and dance about it. In other words, not everyone believes a memorable birthday has to cost an arm and a leg.
components.chinese: 老實說，香港本來就是一個生活成本很高的地方，所以很多人在為一天的慶祝花太多錢之前，都會先三思而後行。除此之外，尤其是年輕人，很多時候更重視氣氛本身，而不是花了多少錢。對他們來說，一頓安靜的晚飯、一個自家做的蛋糕，或者只是和幾個親密朋友一起過，往往比大張旗鼓地鋪張慶祝更有意思。換句話說，並不是每一個人都相信，一個難忘的生日就一定得花很多錢。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: To be honest, Hong Kong is an expensive place to live, so many
people think twice before spending heavily on one single day. On top of that,
younger people in particular often value the atmosphere more than the price
tag. A quiet dinner, a homemade cake, or just spending time with a few close
friends may mean more to them than making a big song and dance about it. In
other words, not everyone believes a memorable birthday has to cost an arm and
a leg.
[老實說，香港本來就是一個生活成本很高的地方，所以很多人在為一天的慶祝花太多錢之
前，都會先三思而後行。除此之外，尤其是年輕人，很多時候更重視氣氛本身，而不是花了
多少錢。對他們來說，一頓安靜的晚飯、一個自家做的蛋糕，或者只是和幾個親密朋友一起
過，往往比大張旗鼓地鋪張慶祝更有意思。換句話說，並不是每一個人都相信，一個難忘的
生日就一定得花很多錢。]

components.english: For instance, some people may simply have a small meal with family, cut a cake at home, and call it a day. That kind of celebration may be modest on the surface, but it can still be warm, meaningful, and more than enough.
components.chinese: 例如，有些人可能只是和家人吃一頓簡單的飯，在家裡切個蛋糕，然後就覺得已經很好了。這種慶祝方式表面上看起來很樸素，但它依然可以很溫暖、很有意義，而且已經綽綽有餘。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, some people may simply have a small meal with family,
cut a cake at home, and call it a day. That kind of celebration may be modest on
the surface, but it can still be warm, meaningful, and more than enough.
[例如，有些人可能只是和家人吃一頓簡單的飯，在家裡切個蛋糕，然後就覺得已經很好
了。這種慶祝方式表面上看起來很樸素，但它依然可以很溫暖、很有意義，而且已經綽綽有

components.english: Overall, I’d say people in Hong Kong can spend quite a lot on birthdays, but it is by no means a hard and fast rule. Some pull out all the stops, while others keep it simple, and in the end it usually comes down to budget, personality, and what the celebration means to them.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說香港人有時的確會在生日上花不少錢，但那絕對不是一條鐵一般的定律。有些人會盡量搞得隆重一點，有些人則喜歡簡單處理；說到底，這通常還是取決於預算、個性，以及那次慶祝對他們有多重要。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say people in Hong Kong can spend quite a lot on
birthdays, but it is by no means a hard and fast rule. Some pull out all the stops,
while others keep it simple, and in the end it usually comes down to budget,
personality, and what the celebration means to them.
[總結來說，我會說香港人有時的確會在生日上花不少錢，但那絕對不是一條鐵一般的定
律。有些人會盡量搞得隆重一點，有些人則喜歡簡單處理；說到底，這通常還是取決於預
算、個性，以及那次慶祝對他們有多重要。]

question.english: Do you think it’s best to do new things on your own or with other people?
question.chinese: 你認為嘗試新事物時，最好自己一個人做，還是和其他人一起做？

source_lines.text: Do you think it’s best to do new things on your own or with other people?
你認為嘗試新事物時，最好自己一個人做，還是和其他人一起做？

components.english: Personally, I think it is usually better to do new things with other people, especially when the experience feels unfamiliar or a little intimidating.
components.chinese: 就我個人而言，我認為嘗試新事物時，通常和其他人一起做會比較好，尤其當那件事很陌生，或者本身有點令人卻步的時候。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: Personally, I think it is usually better to do new things with other
people, especially when the experience feels unfamiliar or a little intimidating.
[就我個人而言，我認為嘗試新事物時，通常和其他人一起做會比較好，尤其當那件事很陌
生，或者本身有點令人卻步的時候。]

components.english: The main reason is that other people can give you support, encouragement, and a bit of courage when you are stepping into the unknown. When people try something new completely alone, they can easily feel out of their depth or start second-guessing themselves. By contrast, doing it with someone else can make the whole experience feel lighter and less daunting. In that sense, company acts a bit like a safety net: it does not remove the challenge, but it makes the first leap easier. On top of that, new experiences are often more enjoyable when you have someone to laugh with, react with, and remember them with afterwards.
components.chinese: 主要原因是，當你走進未知領域時，其他人能給你支持、鼓勵，以及一點勇氣。如果一個人完全獨自去嘗試新事物，他很容易會感到不知所措，或者開始懷疑自己。相比之下，和別人一起做，整個過程往往會顯得輕鬆一點，也沒那麼可怕。從這個角度來看，同伴有點像一張安全網：它不會把挑戰完全拿走，但會讓第一步比較容易跨出去。除此之外，新的體驗很多時候在有人一起笑、一起反應、之後又能一起回味的情況下，也會變得更有樂趣。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The main reason is that other people can give you support,
encouragement, and a bit of courage when you are stepping into the unknown.
When people try something new completely alone, they can easily feel out of
their depth or start second-guessing themselves. By contrast, doing it with
someone else can make the whole experience feel lighter and less daunting. In
that sense, company acts a bit like a safety net: it does not remove the challenge,
but it makes the first leap easier. On top of that, new experiences are often more
enjoyable when you have someone to laugh with, react with, and remember
them with afterwards.
[主要原因是，當你走進未知領域時，其他人能給你支持、鼓勵，以及一點勇氣。如果一個人
完全獨自去嘗試新事物，他很容易會感到不知所措，或者開始懷疑自己。相比之下，和別人
一起做，整個過程往往會顯得輕鬆一點，也沒那麼可怕。從這個角度來看，同伴有點像一張
安全網：它不會把挑戰完全拿走，但會讓第一步比較容易跨出去。除此之外，新的體驗很多
時候在有人一起笑、一起反應、之後又能一起回味的情況下，也會變得更有樂趣。]

components.english: For example, if someone is trying rock climbing, travelling to a new country, or joining a dance class for the first time, going with a friend can make a huge difference. They may still feel nervous, of course, but they are less likely to back out at the last minute. Even if things go slightly wrong, it often turns into a funny memory rather than an uncomfortable one.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一個人第一次去攀石、去陌生國家旅行，或者參加跳舞班，和朋友一起去往往會帶來很大的分別。當然，他仍然可能會緊張，但臨陣退縮的機會通常會少很多。即使過程中出現一點小尷尬，最後很多時也會變成一段好笑的回憶，而不是一段令人不舒服的經歷。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if someone is trying rock climbing, travelling to a new
country, or joining a dance class for the first time, going with a friend can make
a huge difference. They may still feel nervous, of course, but they are less likely
to back out at the last minute. Even if things go slightly wrong, it often turns
into a funny memory rather than an uncomfortable one.
[例如，如果一個人第一次去攀石、去陌生國家旅行，或者參加跳舞班，和朋友一起去往往
會帶來很大的分別。當然，他仍然可能會緊張，但臨陣退縮的機會通常會少很多。即使過程
中出現一點小尷尬，最後很多時也會變成一段好笑的回憶，而不是一段令人不舒服的經

components.english: So, in most cases, I would say doing new things with other people is the better option, because it makes the experience feel safer, more enjoyable, and easier to begin.
components.chinese: 所以，在大多數情況下，我會說和其他人一起嘗試新事物是更好的選擇，因為那會令整個體驗感覺更安心、更有趣，也更容易開始。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, in most cases, I would say doing new things with other people
is the better option, because it makes the experience feel safer, more enjoyable,
and easier to begin.
[所以，在大多數情況下，我會說和其他人一起嘗試新事物是更好的選擇，因為那會令整個
體驗感覺更安心、更有趣，也更容易開始。]

components.english: That said, doing new things on your own also has real value, because it can build independence and self-confidence in a way that group experiences sometimes cannot.
components.chinese: 不過，自己一個人去嘗試新事物也有它真正的價值，因為它能以一種群體經驗未必做到的方式，培養獨立性和自信。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, doing new things on your own also has real value, because it
can build independence and self-confidence in a way that group experiences
sometimes cannot.
[不過，自己一個人去嘗試新事物也有它真正的價值，因為它能以一種群體經驗未必做到的
方式，培養獨立性和自信。]

components.english: When you do something alone, you have no one to hide behind, so you are forced to rely on your own judgment and adapt as you go. That can be uncomfortable at first, but it can also be incredibly rewarding. In a way, solo experiences strip away the training wheels. They show you what you are capable of when there is no one else to lean on. Sometimes that is exactly what a person needs, not because being alone is always better, but because it teaches a quieter, stronger kind of confidence.
components.chinese: 當你一個人去做某件事時，你沒有任何人可以躲在後面，所以你會被迫依靠自己的判斷，並且一路做一路調整。這在一開始可能會讓人不太舒服，但同時也可能非常有收穫。某程度上，獨自進行的經歷就像把輔助輪拿掉一樣。它會讓你看見，在沒有別人可以依靠的情況下，你究竟能做到甚麼。有時候，這正正是一個人需要的，不是因為獨處永遠更好，而是因為它能培養出一種較安靜、卻更扎實的自信。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: When you do something alone, you have no one to hide
behind, so you are forced to rely on your own judgment and adapt as you go.
That can be uncomfortable at first, but it can also be incredibly rewarding. In a
way, solo experiences strip away the training wheels. They show you what you
are capable of when there is no one else to lean on. Sometimes that is exactly
what a person needs, not because being alone is always better, but because it
teaches a quieter, stronger kind of confidence.
[當你一個人去做某件事時，你沒有任何人可以躲在後面，所以你會被迫依靠自己的判斷，
並且一路做一路調整。這在一開始可能會讓人不太舒服，但同時也可能非常有收穫。某程度
上，獨自進行的經歷就像把輔助輪拿掉一樣。它會讓你看見，在沒有別人可以依靠的情況下
，你究竟能做到甚麼。有時候，這正正是一個人需要的，不是因為獨處永遠更好，而是因為
它能培養出一種較安靜、卻更扎實的自信。]

components.english: For instance, if someone takes a solo trip, starts going to the gym alone, or signs up for a course without knowing anyone there, they may feel awkward at first. However, once they get through that initial discomfort, they often come out of it feeling stronger and more self-assured. It is a bit like finding your own footing instead of borrowing someone else’s.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一個人獨自旅行、自己去健身室，或者在完全不認識任何人的情況下報讀一個課程，他一開始可能會覺得很不自在。不過，一旦他撐過了最初那段不適應的階段，往往就會變得更堅定，也更有自信。那感覺有點像是自己站穩腳步，而不是暫時借別人的平衡。

source_lines.text: 7. Example 2: For instance, if someone takes a solo trip, starts going to the gym
alone, or signs up for a course without knowing anyone there, they may feel
awkward at first. However, once they get through that initial discomfort, they
often come out of it feeling stronger and more self-assured. It is a bit like
finding your own footing instead of borrowing someone else’s.
[例如，如果一個人獨自旅行、自己去健身室，或者在完全不認識任何人的情況下報讀一個
課程，他一開始可能會覺得很不自在。不過，一旦他撐過了最初那段不適應的階段，往往就
會變得更堅定，也更有自信。那感覺有點像是自己站穩腳步，而不是暫時借別人的平衡。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say doing new things with other people is usually the easier and more enjoyable way to begin, but doing them alone can be better for personal growth. So, if I had to choose, I would slightly favour doing new things with others first, then doing some of them alone once I feel more ready.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說，和別人一起做新事物，通常是比較容易開始、也比較愉快的方式；不過，自己一個人做，往往更有助個人成長。所以，如果一定要選，我會稍微偏向先和別人一起開始，之後等自己準備得更充足時，再嘗試獨自去做其中一些事。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say doing new things with other people is usually
the easier and more enjoyable way to begin, but doing them alone can be better
for personal growth. So, if I had to choose, I would slightly favour doing new
things with others first, then doing some of them alone once I feel more ready.
[總結來說，我會說，和別人一起做新事物，通常是比較容易開始、也比較愉快的方式；不過
，自己一個人做，往往更有助個人成長。所以，如果一定要選，我會稍微偏向先和別人一起
開始，之後等自己準備得更充足時，再嘗試獨自去做其中一些事。]

question.english: Do you think it’s necessary to spend a lot of money on holding a party?
question.chinese: 你認為辦派對有必要花很多錢嗎？

source_lines.text: Topic: Do you think it’s necessary to spend a lot of money on holding a party?
[主題：你認為辦派對有必要花很多錢嗎？]

components.english: Personally, no, I do not think it is necessary to spend a lot of money on holding a party, because what people usually remember is the atmosphere, not the bill.
components.chinese: 就我個人而言，我不認為辦派對一定有必要花很多錢，因為人們通常記得的是氣氛，而不是帳單。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: Personally, no, I do not think it is necessary to spend a lot of money on
holding a party, because what people usually remember is the atmosphere, not
the bill.
[就我個人而言，我不認為辦派對一定有必要花很多錢，因為人們通常記得的是氣氛，而不
是帳單。]

components.english: A good party usually lives or dies by the people, the mood, and the sense of connection. If the guests feel relaxed, welcomed, and genuinely happy to be there, even a simple gathering can be a great success. By contrast, a party can be expensive on paper but still fall flat if it feels stiff, showy, or emotionally empty. In other words, money can buy the setting, but it cannot manufacture warmth.
components.chinese: 一場好的派對，通常成敗都取決於參與的人、現場的氣氛，以及彼此之間的連結感。如果賓客感到放鬆、受歡迎，而且真的很開心自己來了，那麼即使只是簡單的聚會，也可以非常成功。相比之下，一場派對在表面上也許很昂貴，但如果它讓人感覺拘謹、太過炫耀，或者缺乏感情，那麼它一樣可以很失色。換句話說，金錢可以買到場地和佈置，但買不到真正的溫度。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: A good party usually lives or dies by the people, the mood, and
the sense of connection. If the guests feel relaxed, welcomed, and genuinely
happy to be there, even a simple gathering can be a great success. By contrast, a
party can be expensive on paper but still fall flat if it feels stiff, showy, or
emotionally empty. In other words, money can buy the setting, but it cannot
manufacture warmth.
[一場好的派對，通常成敗都取決於參與的人、現場的氣氛，以及彼此之間的連結感。如果
賓客感到放鬆、受歡迎，而且真的很開心自己來了，那麼即使只是簡單的聚會，也可以非常
成功。相比之下，一場派對在表面上也許很昂貴，但如果它讓人感覺拘謹、太過炫耀，或者
缺乏感情，那麼它一樣可以很失色。換句話說，金錢可以買到場地和佈置，但買不到真正的
溫度。]

components.english: For example, a birthday party at home with homemade food, a few decorations, and close friends can easily be more memorable than a lavish event in a fancy venue. If people are laughing, talking, and enjoying each other’s company, that already does most of the heavy lifting.
components.chinese: 例如，一個在家裡舉行的生日派對，只要有自家準備的食物、一些簡單裝飾，以及幾位親密朋友，往往就可以比在華麗場地舉辦的大型活動更令人難忘。如果大家在一起歡笑、聊天，而且真的享受彼此的陪伴，那其實就已經完成了派對最重要的部分。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, a birthday party at home with homemade food, a few
decorations, and close friends can easily be more memorable than a lavish event
in a fancy venue. If people are laughing, talking, and enjoying each other’s
company, that already does most of the heavy lifting.
[例如，一個在家裡舉行的生日派對，只要有自家準備的食物、一些簡單裝飾，以及幾位親
密朋友，往往就可以比在華麗場地舉辦的大型活動更令人難忘。如果大家在一起歡笑、聊天
，而且真的享受彼此的陪伴，那其實就已經完成了派對最重要的部分。]

components.english: So, in most cases, I would say spending a lot of money is not necessary, because a party is made by people rather than by price tags.
components.chinese: 所以，在大多數情況下，我會說花很多錢並不是必要的，因為真正成就一場派對的，是人，而不是價錢標籤。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, in most cases, I would say spending a lot of money is not
necessary, because a party is made by people rather than by price tags.
[所以，在大多數情況下，我會說花很多錢並不是必要的，因為真正成就一場派對的，是人，
而不是價錢標籤。]

components.english: That said, I do think some occasions may justify spending more, especially if the event is large, formal, or once in a lifetime.
components.chinese: 不過，我也認為某些場合的確值得多花一點錢，尤其是當活動規模較大、較正式，或者一生可能只有一次的時候。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I do think some occasions may justify spending more,
especially if the event is large, formal, or once in a lifetime.
[不過，我也認為某些場合的確值得多花一點錢，尤其是當活動規模較大、較正式，或者一
生可能只有一次的時候。]

components.english: The reason is that some parties carry more weight than others. A wedding, a milestone birthday, or a graduation celebration may involve many guests, better food, a suitable venue, and smoother organisation, so naturally the cost goes up. In those cases, spending more is not necessarily about showing off; it may simply be about making sure the event runs well and feels special. Still, there is a big difference between spending wisely and breaking the bank for the sake of appearances.
components.chinese: 原因是，有些派對本身承載的意義就是比其他聚會更重。一場婚禮、重要的生日，或者畢業慶祝，可能會涉及較多賓客、更好的食物、更合適的場地，以及更周全的安排，所以成本自然會提高。在這些情況下，多花一點錢未必是為了炫耀；它可能只是為了確保整個活動順利，而且真的有特別的感覺。不過，花得明智，和為了面子而花到太過頭，兩者之間其實有很大的分別。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: The reason is that some parties carry more weight than others.
A wedding, a milestone birthday, or a graduation celebration may involve many
guests, better food, a suitable venue, and smoother organisation, so naturally
the cost goes up. In those cases, spending more is not necessarily about showing
off; it may simply be about making sure the event runs well and feels special.
Still, there is a big difference between spending wisely and breaking the bank
for the sake of appearances.
[原因是，有些派對本身承載的意義就是比其他聚會更重。一場婚禮、重要的生日，或者畢
業慶祝，可能會涉及較多賓客、更好的食物、更合適的場地，以及更周全的安排，所以成本
自然會提高。在這些情況下，多花一點錢未必是為了炫耀；它可能只是為了確保整個活動順
利，而且真的有特別的感覺。不過，花得明智，和為了面子而花到太過頭，兩者之間其實有
很大的分別。]

components.english: For instance, if a couple are holding a wedding for a large family, they may need to spend more on seating, catering, and photography. That can make sense. But if someone pours money into every little detail just to impress other people, then the party may start to feel more like a performance than a celebration.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一對新人要為大家庭舉辦婚禮，他們可能就真的需要在座位安排、餐飲，以及攝影方面花比較多的錢，這是合理的。但如果一個人把錢灌進每一個細節，只是為了讓別人覺得厲害，那麼那場派對就可能慢慢變得比較像一場表演，而不是一場慶祝。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, if a couple are holding a wedding for a large family,
they may need to spend more on seating, catering, and photography. That can
make sense. But if someone pours money into every little detail just to impress
other people, then the party may start to feel more like a performance than a
celebration.
[例如，如果一對新人要為大家庭舉辦婚禮，他們可能就真的需要在座位安排、餐飲，以及
攝影方面花比較多的錢，這是合理的。但如果一個人把錢灌進每一個細節，只是為了讓別人
覺得厲害，那麼那場派對就可能慢慢變得比較像一場表演，而不是一場慶祝。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say it is not necessary to spend a lot of money on a party unless the occasion genuinely calls for it. To me，what matters most is not how much money goes in, but how much joy comes out.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說，除非那個場合本身真的有需要，否則辦派對並不一定要花很多錢。最重要的不是投進去多少錢，而是最後帶出了多少快樂。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say it is not necessary to spend a lot of money on a
party unless the occasion genuinely calls for it. To me，what matters most is not
how much money goes in, but how much joy comes out.
[總結來說，我會說，除非那個場合本身真的有需要，否則辦派對並不一定要花很多錢。最
重要的不是投進去多少錢，而是最後帶出了多少快樂。]

question.english: Do people in your country usually go out to celebrate traditional festivals with others?
question.chinese: 你所在的地方，人們通常會和其他人一起外出慶祝傳統節日嗎？

source_lines.text: Topic: Do people in your country usually go out to celebrate traditional festivals with others?
你所在的地方，人們通常會和其他人一起外出慶祝傳統節日嗎？

components.english: Yes, definitely. In Hong Kong, people very often celebrate traditional festivals with others, especially during big occasions like Lunar New Year, Mid-Autumn Festival, and the Dragon Boat Festival.
components.chinese: 是的，當然會。在香港，人們很常和其他人一起慶祝傳統節日，尤其是在農曆新年、中秋節和端午節這些大節日的時候。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: Yes, definitely. In Hong Kong, people very often celebrate traditional
festivals with others, especially during big occasions like Lunar New Year,
Mid-Autumn Festival, and the Dragon Boat Festival.
[是的，當然會。在香港，人們很常和其他人一起慶祝傳統節日，尤其是在農曆新年、中秋節
和端午節這些大節日的時候。]

components.english: I think the main reason is that these festivals are social by nature. They are not really the kind of occasions people keep to themselves. Very often, the festive mood spills out of the home and into the streets. People gather for reunion meals, visit flower markets, watch performances, or simply go out to soak up the atmosphere together.
components.chinese: 我覺得主要原因是，這些節日本身就很有社交性。它們並不是那種人們會自己靜靜過的節日。很多時候，節日氣氛不只是留在家裡，而是會自然地流到街上。人們會一起吃團年飯、逛花市、看表演，或者單純一起外出感受節日氣氛。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: I think the main reason is that these festivals are social by
nature. They are not really the kind of occasions people keep to themselves. Very
often, the festive mood spills out of the home and into the streets. People gather
for reunion meals, visit flower markets, watch performances, or simply go out to
soak up the atmosphere together.
[我覺得主要原因是，這些節日本身就很有社交性。它們並不是那種人們會自己靜靜過的節
日。很多時候，節日氣氛不只是留在家裡，而是會自然地流到街上。人們會一起吃團年飯、
逛花市、看表演，或者單純一起外出感受節日氣氛。]

components.english: For example, during Lunar New Year, many families go to flower markets, watch lion or dragon dances, and enjoy public celebrations like the night parade or fireworks. During Mid-Autumn Festival, people often gather with family or friends to admire the moon, carry lanterns, or watch the Tai Hang Fire Dragon Dance.
components.chinese: 例如，在農曆新年期間，很多家庭會去逛花市、看舞獅或舞龍，也會參與像新年花車巡遊或煙花這類公開慶祝活動。到了中秋節，人們又常常會和家人或朋友一起賞月、玩燈籠，或者去看大坑舞火龍。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, during Lunar New Year, many families go to flower
markets, watch lion or dragon dances, and enjoy public celebrations like the
night parade or fireworks. During Mid-Autumn Festival, people often gather
with family or friends to admire the moon, carry lanterns, or watch the Tai
Hang Fire Dragon Dance.
[例如，在農曆新年期間，很多家庭會去逛花市、看舞獅或舞龍，也會參與像新年花車巡遊
或煙花這類公開慶祝活動。到了中秋節，人們又常常會和家人或朋友一起賞月、玩燈籠，或
者去看大坑舞火龍。]

components.english: So yes, in general, people in Hong Kong do tend to go out and celebrate traditional festivals with others, because these occasions are as much about togetherness as they are about tradition.
components.chinese: 所以，是的，一般來說，香港人確實很傾向和其他人一起外出慶祝傳統節日，因為這些節日不只是關於傳統本身，也同樣關乎團聚和一起過節的感覺。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So yes, in general, people in Hong Kong do tend to go out and
celebrate traditional festivals with others, because these occasions are as much
about togetherness as they are about tradition.
[所以，是的，一般來說，香港人確實很傾向和其他人一起外出慶祝傳統節日，因為這些節
日不只是關於傳統本身，也同樣關乎團聚和一起過節的感覺。]

components.english: That said, not every festival celebration is loud or public, because some of them are much more family-centred.
components.chinese: 不過，也不是每一種節日慶祝方式都很熱鬧或者很公開，因為有些節日其實更偏向以家庭為中心。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, not every festival celebration is loud or public, because some
of them are much more family-centred.
[不過，也不是每一種節日慶祝方式都很熱鬧或者很公開，因為有些節日其實更偏向以家庭
為中心。]

components.english: For some people, the heart of the festival is not the big public event, but the time spent with relatives at home or around the dinner table. In other words, people may still celebrate with others, just in a quieter way. It is less about going all out in public and more about keeping family ties warm and alive.
components.chinese: 對某些人來說，節日最重要的核心並不是那些大型公開活動，而是和親人在家裡，或者圍著飯桌相聚的時光。換句話說，人們仍然是和別人一起慶祝，只不過方式比較安靜而已。重點未必是熱熱鬧鬧地外出，而是把家人之間的聯繫維繫得更溫暖、更有感情。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: For some people, the heart of the festival is not the big public
event, but the time spent with relatives at home or around the dinner table. In
other words, people may still celebrate with others, just in a quieter way. It is
less about going all out in public and more about keeping family ties warm and
alive.
[對某些人來說，節日最重要的核心並不是那些大型公開活動，而是和親人在家裡，或者圍
著飯桌相聚的時光。換句話說，人們仍然是和別人一起慶祝，只不過方式比較安靜而已。重
點未必是熱熱鬧鬧地外出，而是把家人之間的聯繫維繫得更溫暖、更有感情。]

components.english: For instance, on Chinese New Year’s Eve, many families gather for a reunion dinner, and during Mid-Autumn Festival they may share mooncakes and spend time together before or after going out. So, even when people do not join a huge public celebration, they are still very likely to celebrate with others.
components.chinese: 例如，在農曆新年前夕，很多家庭都會一起吃團年飯；而在中秋節，他們也可能會先一起吃月餅、相聚聊天，然後才外出，或者外出之後再回家團聚。所以，即使有些人沒有參加特別大型的公開活動，他們其實仍然很大機會是和其他人一起過節的。

source_lines.text: 7. Example 2: For instance, on Chinese New Year’s Eve, many families gather for
a reunion dinner, and during Mid-Autumn Festival they may share mooncakes
and spend time together before or after going out. So, even when people do not
join a huge public celebration, they are still very likely to celebrate with others.
[例如，在農曆新年前夕，很多家庭都會一起吃團年飯；而在中秋節，他們也可能會先一起
吃月餅、相聚聊天，然後才外出，或者外出之後再回家團聚。所以，即使有些人沒有參加特
別大型的公開活動，他們其實仍然很大機會是和其他人一起過節的。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say yes, people in Hong Kong usually celebrate traditional festivals with others, but the form that takes can vary. Sometimes it is a lively public event, and sometimes it is just a family table full of food, laughter, and tradition.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說，是的，香港人通常會和其他人一起慶祝傳統節日，只不過形式可以很不一樣。有時候，那會是一場熱熱鬧鬧的公開活動；有時候，則只是圍著一張擺滿食物、笑聲和傳統的家庭飯桌。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say yes, people in Hong Kong usually celebrate
traditional festivals with others, but the form that takes can vary. Sometimes it
is a lively public event, and sometimes it is just a family table full of food,
laughter, and tradition.
[總結來說，我會說，是的，香港人通常會和其他人一起慶祝傳統節日，只不過形式可以很
不一樣。有時候，那會是一場熱熱鬧鬧的公開活動；有時候，則只是圍著一張擺滿食物、笑
聲和傳統的家庭飯桌。]

question.english: Is family important in your country?
question.chinese: 你認為在你的國家，家庭重要嗎？

source_lines.text: Topic: Is family important in your country?
你認為在你的國家，家庭重要嗎？

components.english: Yes, very much so. I’d say family is still one of the most important parts of life in Hong Kong.
components.chinese: 是的，非常重要。我會說，在香港，家庭仍然是生活中最重要的部分之一。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: Yes, very much so. I’d say family is still one of the most important parts
of life in Hong Kong.
[是的，非常重要。我會說，在香港，家庭仍然是生活中最重要的部分之一。]

components.english: The main reason is that family is often seen as both an emotional anchor and a practical source of support. When life gets stressful, people usually turn to their family first, whether they need advice, comfort, or help making an important decision. In that sense, family is not just part of the background; it is more like the backbone of everyday life. It shapes people’s values, gives them a sense of belonging, and reminds them where home really is.
components.chinese: 主要原因是，家庭往往同時被視為情感上的依靠，以及實際支援的來源。當生活變得有壓力時，人們通常會先想到家人，無論他們需要的是建議、安慰，還是幫助自己作出重要決定。從這個角度來看，家庭並不是生活的背景板，而更像是日常生活的骨幹。它塑造人的價值觀，給人歸屬感，也提醒人真正的家在哪裡。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The main reason is that family is often seen as both an
emotional anchor and a practical source of support. When life gets stressful,
people usually turn to their family first, whether they need advice, comfort, or
help making an important decision. In that sense, family is not just part of the
background; it is more like the backbone of everyday life. It shapes people’s
values, gives them a sense of belonging, and reminds them where home really is.
[主要原因是，家庭往往同時被視為情感上的依靠，以及實際支援的來源。當生活變得有壓
力時，人們通常會先想到家人，無論他們需要的是建議、安慰，還是幫助自己作出重要決
定。從這個角度來看，家庭並不是生活的背景板，而更像是日常生活的骨幹。它塑造人的價
值觀，給人歸屬感，也提醒人真正的家在哪裡。]

components.english: For example, in Hong Kong it is very common for parents to support their children well into adulthood, especially with education, housing, or career choices. At the same time, many adult children feel a strong responsibility to look after their parents as they grow older. So family ties often work both ways: people lean on one another, and that support can make a real difference.
components.chinese: 例如，在香港，父母在孩子長大成人之後，仍然繼續在教育、住屋，或者職業選擇上支持他們，是很常見的事。與此同時，很多成年子女也會對照顧年長父母有很強的責任感。所以，家庭聯繫往往是雙向的：人們彼此依靠，而這種支持往往會帶來很大的不同。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, in Hong Kong it is very common for parents to
support their children well into adulthood, especially with education, housing,
or career choices. At the same time, many adult children feel a strong
responsibility to look after their parents as they grow older. So family ties often
work both ways: people lean on one another, and that support can make a real
difference.
[例如，在香港，父母在孩子長大成人之後，仍然繼續在教育、住屋，或者職業選擇上支持他
們，是很常見的事。與此同時，很多成年子女也會對照顧年長父母有很強的責任感。所以，
家庭聯繫往往是雙向的：人們彼此依靠，而這種支持往往會帶來很大的不同。]

components.english: So yes, I would definitely say family is important, because it provides people with support, stability, and a sense of identity.
components.chinese: 所以，是的，我肯定會說家庭是重要的，因為它給人支持、穩定感，以及一種身份認同。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So yes, I would definitely say family is important, because it
provides people with support, stability, and a sense of identity.
[所以，是的，我肯定會說家庭是重要的，因為它給人支持、穩定感，以及一種身份認同。]

components.english: That said, I do think the way people relate to family is changing, especially among younger generations.
components.chinese: 不過，我的確認為，人們和家庭相處的方式正在改變，特別是在年輕一代之中。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I do think the way people relate to family is changing,
especially among younger generations.
[不過，我的確認為，人們和家庭相處的方式正在改變，特別是在年輕一代之中。]

components.english: Life in Hong Kong is fast, expensive, and often exhausting, so people do not always have as much time as they would like for family life. On top of that, younger people today tend to value independence more strongly than in the past. So although family still matters deeply, it may not always be expressed in the same traditional way. In other words, the bond is still there, but it can look a little different on the surface.
components.chinese: 香港的生活節奏很快，開支也很高，而且往往令人疲憊不堪，所以人們未必總有自己理想中的那麼多時間去經營家庭生活。另外，今天的年輕人通常比過去更重視獨立。因此，雖然家庭依然非常重要，但它未必總是以傳統方式表現出來。換句話說，那份連結仍然存在，只不過在表面上看起來可能有點不一樣。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: Life in Hong Kong is fast, expensive, and often exhausting, so
people do not always have as much time as they would like for family life. On
top of that, younger people today tend to value independence more strongly
than in the past. So although family still matters deeply, it may not always be
expressed in the same traditional way. In other words, the bond is still there,
but it can look a little different on the surface.
[香港的生活節奏很快，開支也很高，而且往往令人疲憊不堪，所以人們未必總有自己理想
中的那麼多時間去經營家庭生活。另外，今天的年輕人通常比過去更重視獨立。因此，雖然
家庭依然非常重要，但它未必總是以傳統方式表現出來。換句話說，那份連結仍然存在，只
不過在表面上看起來可能有點不一樣。]

components.english: For instance, some young adults may want more privacy, choose to move out, or make their own lifestyle choices more freely. Even so, when it comes to major moments such as illness, career changes, or marriage, many of them still turn back to family. When it really matters, family is often where the heart goes first.
components.chinese: 例如，有些年輕成年人可能更想要私人空間、選擇搬出去住，或者更自由地作出自己的生活選擇。即使如此，當遇到生病、轉工，或者結婚這些重大時刻時，很多人仍然會回到家庭那邊尋求支持。到了真正重要的時候，人的心往往還是會先走向家人。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, some young adults may want more privacy, choose to
move out, or make their own lifestyle choices more freely. Even so, when it
comes to major moments such as illness, career changes, or marriage, many of
them still turn back to family. When it really matters, family is often where the
heart goes first.
[例如，有些年輕成年人可能更想要私人空間、選擇搬出去住，或者更自由地作出自己的生
活選擇。即使如此，當遇到生病、轉工，或者結婚這些重大時刻時，很多人仍然會回到家庭
那邊尋求支持。到了真正重要的時候，人的心往往還是會先走向家人。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say family remains extremely important in Hong Kong, even if the form it takes is gradually evolving. The routines may change, the roles may shift, but the emotional thread still runs deep.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說家庭在香港仍然非常重要，即使它呈現出來的形式正在慢慢改變。日常相處模式也許會變，家庭角色也許會移動，但那條情感上的線仍然牽得很深。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say family remains extremely important in Hong
Kong, even if the form it takes is gradually evolving. The routines may change,
the roles may shift, but the emotional thread still runs deep.
[總結來說，我會說家庭在香港仍然非常重要，即使它呈現出來的形式正在慢慢改變。日常
相處模式也許會變，家庭角色也許會移動，但那條情感上的線仍然牽得很深。]

question.english: Who should be responsible for caring for the elderly: the family or the government?
question.chinese: 照顧長者的責任應該由誰承擔：家庭還是政府？

source_lines.text: Who should be responsible for caring for the elderly: the family or the government?
主題：照顧長者的責任應該由誰承擔：家庭還是政府？

components.english: Personally, I think both should be responsible, but if I had to choose one side, I would say the government should play the bigger role overall.
components.chinese: 就我個人而言，我認為雙方都應該負責，不過如果一定要選一邊的話，我會說整體上政府應該承擔較大的責任。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: Personally, I think both should be responsible, but if I had to choose one
side, I would say the government should play the bigger role overall.
[就我個人而言，我認為雙方都應該負責，不過如果一定要選一邊的話，我會說整體上政府
應該承擔較大的責任。]

components.english: The main reason is that elderly care is too important, too costly, and too demanding to be left entirely to individual families. Love and responsibility matter a great deal, of course, but love alone does not pay for medical treatment, professional care, or long-term support. In many cases, families are already stretched thin with work, childcare, and financial pressure, so asking them to carry the whole burden on their own can be simply too much. In that sense, the government should act as the safety net, because basic dignity in old age should not depend purely on whether someone happens to have a strong family behind them.
components.chinese: 主要原因是，長者照顧這件事太重要、太花錢，也太需要持續投入，所以不能完全交給個別家庭自己承擔。當然，愛和責任感都非常重要，但單靠愛，並不能支付醫療費用、專業照護，或者長期支援的成本。在很多情況下，家庭本身已經被工作、育兒，以及經濟壓力拉得很緊了，如果還要他們獨自扛起全部責任，往往會太沉重。從這個角度來說，政府應該成為那張安全網，因為一個人在老年是否能活得有基本尊嚴，不應該純粹取決於他剛好有沒有一個很強的家庭支撐。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The main reason is that elderly care is too important, too costly,
and too demanding to be left entirely to individual families. Love and
responsibility matter a great deal, of course, but love alone does not pay for
medical treatment, professional care, or long-term support. In many cases,
families are already stretched thin with work, childcare, and financial pressure,
so asking them to carry the whole burden on their own can be simply too much.
In that sense, the government should act as the safety net, because basic dignity
in old age should not depend purely on whether someone happens to have a
strong family behind them.
[主要原因是，長者照顧這件事太重要、太花錢，也太需要持續投入，所以不能完全交給個
別家庭自己承擔。當然，愛和責任感都非常重要，但單靠愛，並不能支付醫療費用、專業照
護，或者長期支援的成本。在很多情況下，家庭本身已經被工作、育兒，以及經濟壓力拉得
很緊了，如果還要他們獨自扛起全部責任，往往會太沉重。從這個角度來說，政府應該成為
那張安全網，因為一個人在老年是否能活得有基本尊嚴，不應該純粹取決於他剛好有沒有
一個很強的家庭支撐。]

components.english: For example, if an elderly person needs regular hospital visits, rehabilitation, or even residential care, their children may want to help, but they may not have the time, money, or professional knowledge to do everything themselves. In that kind of situation, public healthcare, subsidies, and community services can make all the difference.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一位長者需要定期覆診、做復康，甚至入住護理院舍，他的子女也許很想幫忙，但未必有足夠時間、金錢，或者專業知識去把所有事情都一手包辦。在這種情況下，公共醫療、津貼，以及社區支援服務，就可能帶來很大的分別。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if an elderly person needs regular hospital visits,
rehabilitation, or even residential care, their children may want to help, but
they may not have the time, money, or professional knowledge to do everything
themselves. In that kind of situation, public healthcare, subsidies, and
community services can make all the difference.
[例如，如果一位長者需要定期覆診、做復康，甚至入住護理院舍，他的子女也許很想幫忙，
但未必有足夠時間、金錢，或者專業知識去把所有事情都一手包辦。在這種情況下，公共醫
療、津貼，以及社區支援服務，就可能帶來很大的分別。]

components.english: So, if I had to put it simply, I would say the government should take primary responsibility, because elderly care is not just a private matter; it is a social responsibility as well.
components.chinese: 所以，如果要我簡單地說，我會認為政府應該承擔主要責任，因為照顧長者不只是私人家庭的事情，同時也是整個社會的責任。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, if I had to put it simply, I would say the government should
take primary responsibility, because elderly care is not just a private matter; it is
a social responsibility as well.
[所以，如果要我簡單地說，我會認為政府應該承擔主要責任，因為照顧長者不只是私人家
庭的事情，同時也是整個社會的責任。]

components.english: That said, I do not think families can just step back, because emotional support and daily companionship are things the government can never fully replace.
